attributes.stem:
A 72-year-old man presents to the orthopaedic ward following an elective left total knee replacement performed yesterday. He has a medical history of osteoarthritis affecting multiple joints, hypertension, and type 2 diabetes. His regular medications include metformin, perindopril, and paracetamol. He has no known drug allergies.

On examination, he appears comfortable at rest. His pulse rate is 78/min, blood pressure 135/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 97% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. His left knee dressing is clean and dry. He reports severe pain (8/10) on attempting to mobilise with the physiotherapist despite regular paracetamol.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate analgesic to add?
options: Referral for a peripheral nerve block
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with breakthrough pain during mobilisation after total knee replacement surgery. While regular paracetamol provides good background analgesia, additional pain relief is clearly needed to facilitate early mobilisation, which is crucial for optimal post-operative outcomes.

Immediate-release oxycodone as needed is the most appropriate choice. It allows flexible dosing based on pain severity, particularly during periods of increased activity such as physiotherapy sessions. The immediate-release formulation provides rapid onset of action when needed, without unnecessarily exposing the patient to continuous opioid effects.

Regular slow-release oxycodone would be excessive for breakthrough pain and could lead to unnecessary sedation and other opioid-related side effects during periods when the patient is comfortable at rest.

Regular slow-release tramadol is less effective than oxycodone for acute post-operative pain and has potential for interactions with other medications in elderly patients. It also has a slower onset of action, making it less suitable for breakthrough pain.

Referral for a peripheral nerve block would be invasive and unnecessary at this stage, given that the pain is adequately controlled at rest and simple oral analgesia options have not been fully explored.

Immediate-release tramadol as needed is less effective than oxycodone for acute post-operative pain as it has a more variable and unpredictable metabolization pathway, as it utilises the CYP2D6 pathway to go from a pro-drug in to an opioid. Trials suggest in the post-operative patient cohort Tramadol does not have a superior safety profile to oxycodone and is associated with more all cause serious adverse events.

The key principle in post-operative pain management is using regular simple analgesics (like paracetamol) as background analgesia, with the addition of as-needed stronger analgesics for breakthrough pain. This approach allows optimal pain control while minimising side effects.
learningPoints: Regular paracetamol plus as-needed opioids is the standard approach to post-operative pain management after total knee replacement.
Early mobilisation after total knee replacement is crucial for optimal outcomes and prevention of complications.
Immediate-release oxycodone as needed allows flexible dosing based on activity levels and pain severity.
Pain that interferes with mobilisation after joint replacement requires prompt intervention to prevent complications.
references: Ferreira GE, Patanwala AE, Turton H, Langford AV, Harris IA, Maher CG, McLachlan AJ, Glare P, Lin CW. How is postoperative pain after hip and knee replacement managed? An analysis of two large hospitals in Australia. Perioperative Medicine. 2024 May 31;13(1):49. Available online at ; https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s13741-024-00403-w

Bosco E, Riester MR, Beaudoin FL, Schoenfeld AJ, Gravenstein S, Mor V, Zullo AR. Comparative safety of tramadol and other opioids following total hip and knee arthroplasty. BMC geriatrics. 2024 Apr 5;24(1):319. Available online at ; https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s12877-024-04933-2
